figures.gloss: In Mesoamerican thought, the self is doubled: the tonal — the day-sign soul, one's calendrical destiny and waking person — and the nagual, the hidden animal co-essence, or the specialist able to cross into it and travel apart from the body.
figures.facet: The best-documented Mesoamerican answer to what else a human is: a public calendrical person set beside a hidden animal counterpart — the schema the famous forgery imitated.
figures.provenance: Nahua tonalli in Alfredo López Austin, 'The Human Body and Ideology' (1980); nagualism surveyed in George Foster, 'Nagualism in Mexico and Guatemala' (1944); colonial attestation in Ruiz de Alarcón's 'Treatise' (1629). Distinct from Castaneda's fabricated version (the forgery node); Horden's modern Toltec I Ching synthesis rides the yin-yang bridge edge, not this entry's pedigree.

figures.gloss: The rubber-ball game played on an I-shaped court: a cosmic contest of life against death, sun against underworld, enacted with human bodies — and sometimes settled with human blood.
figures.facet: Not a metaphor performed but a rite enacted: at this game the Hero Twins out-played death itself and rose as the sun and the moon.
figures.provenance: The cosmic ballgame and the Hero-Twins myth are firmly attested (Popol Vuh). A distinct game from patolli — the athletic court, not the four-directions board.

edges.note: The Twins win their lives by out-playing death at the court — the founding match of the Popol Vuh.
William Douglas Horden's The Toltec I Ching joins the Mesoamerican tonal/nagual to the Chinese yin/yang — a real synthesis from a real lineage, the join this whole project is named for.
The famous version is fabricated. Its seam is aimed deliberately back at the grounded Toltec line it imitated — the fake set beside the real so the difference can be seen.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The generation of life and cosmic continuation through highly orchestrated, high-stakes ritual collision that demands a blood price.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems partition the human entity into distinct, operating spiritual and animal components that interact across different planes of reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems feature an intimate, inextricably linked spiritual double or divine counterpart that the practitioner must integrate with to achieve ultimate realization.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems mathematically divide time to map birth horoscopes, determining an individual's fate and psychological composition based on calendrical geometry.
A recurring ritual that re-enacts the founding passage through death to renew the covenant of the world; a distant structural parallel, flagged.
GOLD SEAM: A vital spirit double or co-essence that is intimately tied to the practitioner's fate, power, and spiritual maturation.

Ritual re-enactment as cosmic maintenance: the purulli recitation of the serpent's defeat and the Mesoamerican ballgame both stage a mythic combat to keep the world's order turning — independent inventions of the same logic.

GOLD SEAM: the chi and the Mesoamerican tonal are both a destiny-companion-spirit bound to a person from birth — a portion of self carried in the unseen. Independent invention across families; both seam to the proposed personal-daimon joint.
The shaman's detachable soul that journeys and can be lost rhymes with the Mesoamerican nagual — the self that travels apart from the body.

Both involve the idea of personal destiny and choice — but reshut is the free will to choose good or evil, while tonal is the day-soul that shapes one's fate. A loose parallel of human agency, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel where one's own life force is inextricably bound and conditionally linked to the fate of a superior or external entity.
GOLD SEAM: Lethal, highly choreographed athletic rituals performed in central courtyards that act as cosmic sacraments rather than mere public sports.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions posit an inextricable spiritual and energetic link between a human being and a specific animal counterpart that provides vitality and protection.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Olmec 'were-animal' motif is the foundational root of the pan-Mesoamerican belief in animal spirit companions and shamanic transformation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The rulers depicted in the colossal heads wear the thick protective helmets utilized in the sacred Mesoamerican ballgame.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Extreme, potentially lethal physical athletic competition employed to resolve intense political and cosmic tensions among rival factions.

Both name a second, hidden register of the self that the practitioner crosses into — the San healer in trance and the Mesoamerican nagual-state — the everyday self set against its potent other. (Joe's truth-bar: the San dance is grounded ethnography; the kinship is structural, and the Mesoamerican side carries its own forgery caveat.)
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A binary reality model where the visible, material world is viewed as a dependent shadow of underlying, invisible spiritual forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A structural parallel where the practitioner maps and manipulates the fluid subjective realm (Nagual) to force necessary changes in the fixed objective world (Tonal).

dossier.essence: The shared religious civilization of Mesoamerica — the cultural sphere running from the Olmec (from c. 1200 BCE) through the Maya and the Aztec to the Spanish Conquest of 1521 — treated here as the common substrate underlying its many local cults. Across two and a half millennia and unrelated languages, its peoples shared a distinctive complex: interlocking sacred calendars, a maize-based creation, the feathered serpent, the storm-and-rain god, the ballgame, the quartered cosmos, and the conviction that the gods must be fed with human offering.
dossier.cosmos: Reality is a quartered plane — four directions, each with its color and patron, around a central world-tree (the ceiba) — stacked with layered heavens above and an underworld below (Mictlan, Xibalba). Time is cyclical and world-destroying: successive world-ages or 'suns' rise and fall, and the day itself, tracked by the meshed 260-day and 365-day counts (the 52-year Calendar Round), is a living force that shapes fate. A single dynamic sacred energy — teotl in Nahuatl — pervades all things and is manifested through the gods and their human vessels (ixiptla).
dossier.practice: A trained priesthood kept the calendars and divined by them; ritual centered on human sacrifice and autosacrificial bloodletting, deity-impersonation, the ballgame, pilgrimage, and monumental ceremonial architecture. Ordinary life was governed by the day-count through naming, divination, and the festival cycle. Public cults ended with the Conquest, but calendrical and devotional practice persists in folk-Catholic form.
dossier.canon: No alphabetic scripture: pre-Columbian screenfold codices (the Borgia group, the Dresden Codex), monumental inscriptions, and colonial transcriptions of oral tradition — above all the K'iche' Popol Vuh and Sahagún's Florentine Codex.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the 'one through many' — a single sacred energy (teotl) diffused through countless masked gods and human embodiments — coupled to sacred time as the machinery of destiny.
sources: Miguel León-Portilla, 'Aztec Thought and Culture: A Study of the Ancient Nahuatl Mind' (1963)
Alfredo López Austin, 'The Human Body and Ideology: Concepts of the Ancient Nahuas' (1988)
Mary Miller & Karl Taube, 'An Illustrated Dictionary of the Gods and Symbols of Ancient Mexico and the Maya' (1993)
